summary: Gruber responds to readers comparing Apple's 2010 HTC patent suit to the old Apple v. Microsoft 'look and feel' copyright case. He notes the legal distinction between copyright and patent cases and that the Microsoft suit happened entirely during Jobs's exile. He then poses a thought experiment: if Apple had patented the Mac's GUI innovations in 1984, it wouldn't just have blocked Microsoft — it would have blocked NeXT, the company Jobs founded. The post is a concise argument against aggressive patent enforcement, concluding that good ideas are meant to spread.
key_points: The Apple v. Microsoft case was a copyright suit, not a patent case — legally an entirely different ballgame
Both disputes share the same fundamental pattern: Apple creates a revolutionary product, competitors adopt the same ideas
If Apple had patented its 1984 Mac GUI innovations, NeXT (Steve Jobs's own company) would also have been blocked from using them
Some Apple supporters want the company to use patents to prevent a repeat of the Windows situation, but Gruber is skeptical of that approach
money_quotes: I think it's fair to say that's an entirely different ballgame legally.
Apple brings to market a revolutionary next step in personal computers; competitors then use those same ideas in competing products. Microsoft and Windows then; Google and Android now.
Apple got screwed the last time when a competitor was able to shamelessly use the ideas that Apple first created, and so Apple should do whatever it can to keep that from happening again.
But what if Apple had patented these things in 1984, and had successfully protected these patents from being used by other U.S. companies?
It's not just Microsoft that would've been blocked from creating Windows as we know it. A company called NeXT would have been blocked from creating NeXTStep.
Every single Mac feature I described above was part of the NeXT UI as well.
Good ideas are meant to spread.
key_insight: If Apple had successfully locked down its GUI patents in 1984, it would have blocked not just Microsoft but also NeXT — proving that overzealous IP protection can backfire on the innovator themselves.
